segments.text: Bacterial Genetics

R. Massini 1907
“E. coli mutable”
Lactose negative strain-unable to ferment lactose
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK21942/

S. Luria and M. Delbruck (1943)
Mutations of Bacteria
from Virus Sensitivity to Virus Resistance

Nomenclature for bacterial mutations
Prototrophs
can grow on minimal media
Auxotrophs
auxilium means help
need additional nutrients in media
examples:
met, bio, thr, leu
generally use E. coli strains with 2 or 	more 	mutations to prevent reversions

Lederberg and Tatum in 1946
met- bio- thr+ leu+ thi+    X  met+ bio + thr-leu-thi-
wild type
Not back mutations (use double mutant-reversion really unlikely)
Frequency of recombination was 1 x 10-7 or 1 in 10,000,000

Important Concepts
The chromosome of a bacteria is basically 	haploid
circular
(extra-chromosomal elements may be polyploid)
Bacteria can exchange DNA by three fundamentally different mechanisms, conjugation, transformation, and transduction

Gene Transfer in Prokaryotes
Conjugation
bacterial mating
Transformation
bacteria take up naked DNA
Transduction
viral transfer of DNA

Conjugation
A process having a unidirectional transfer of genetic information through direct cellular contact between a donor (“male”) and a recipient (“female”) bacterial cell

Plasmid
An extrachromosomal genetic element consisting of double stranded DNA that replicates autonomously from the host chromosome
F Plasmid (Fertility Factor)

Example of Plasmid Cloning Vector
For E. coli
EcoR1

F plasmid replicates in cytoplasm of F+ cell
F= Fertility factor

F+ Cell has the F plasmid
F- Cell does not have the F plasmid
F+ cells cannot conjugate with other F+ cells
Nomenclature and Characteristics of E. coli cells with respect to F plasmid
F+ can be transferred to an F- cell

F Plasmid
Transfer Origin
Genes here get transferred first           Genes over here get transferred last

Conjugation Variations
F+  /  F-
Hfr
F’

Hfr
High frequency of recombination
Cells possessing the F plasmid integrated into the host chromosome, will try to mate with F- cells, and transfer portions of the F+ host genome.
F Plasmid can integrate into host genome by recombination
Generation of Hfr
Hfr transfers portions of E. coli chromosome (never the entire F plasmid)

Circular F Plasmid
E. coli
E. coli
F plasmid recombined into E. coli genome
100 kb
4,639 kb
Hfr brings in new genes, which can recombine into host genome
Interrupted mating can be used to determine linear order of genes in bacteria
F plasmid can recombine into many places in E. coli genome, creating many different Hfr strains
Typically, the linear order is first determined by interrupted mating, and then fine structure mapping is addressed through recombination mapping

Conjugation Variations
F+
Hfr
F’
Generation of F’
F’ transfers portions of E. coli chromosome

Summary of Conjugation Variations
F+
Hfr
F’

E. coli genome structure-circular, haploid
Should be able to do problems involving interrupted mating   	in E. coli

With the technique of interrupted mating four Hfr strains were tested for the sequence in which they transmitted a number of different genes to an F- strain.  Each Hfr strain was found to transmit its genes is a unique sequence, as shown in the accompanying table.  What is the gene sequence in the original strain from which these Hfr strains were derived?  Is the genome linear or circular?
Hfr strain
Order of transmission	1	2	3	4
First			Z	R	Z	E
F	H	G	M
B	M	X	H
A	E	C	R
E	A	R	C
Last			M	B	H	X
